S : Sometimes a United States dollar is worth 12 pesos in Mexico. S 6 : Another time it may be worth eight pesos. The Proper sequence should be:
explanation: No answer description is available.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: S 1 : Moncure Conway devoted his life to two great objects freedom of thought, and freedom of the individual. P : They threaten both kinds of freedom. Q : But something also has been lost. R : There are now dangers, somewhat different in form from those of the past ages. S : In regard to both these objects, something has been gained since his time. S 6 : Unless a vigorous and vigilant public opinion can be aroused in defence of them, there will be much less of both a hundred years hence then there is now. The Proper sequence should be:
explanation: No answer description is available.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: S 1 : The study of speech disorders due to brain injury suggests that patients can think without having adequate control over their language. P : But they succeed in playing games of chess. Q : Some patients, for example fail to find the names of objects presented to them. R : They can even use the concepts needed for chess playing, though they are unable to express many of the concepts in ordinary language. S : They even find it difficult to interpret long written notices. S 6 : How they manage to do this we do not know. The Proper sequence should be:
explanation: No answer description is available.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: S 1 : A black haired, young woman came tripping along. P : She was leading a young woman wearing a hat. Q : The woman swept it off and tossed it in the air. R : The child jumped up to catch the hat. S : The young man tossed his head to shake the hat back. S 6 : Both disappeared from view. The Proper sequence should be:
explanation: No answer description is available.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: S 1 : We now know that the oceans are very deep. P : For example, the Indian ocean has a range called the Indian Ridge. Q : Much of it is fairly flat. R : However, there are great mountain ranges as well. S : On average the bottom is 2.5 miles to 3.5 miles down S 6 : This reaches from the India to the Antarctic. The Proper sequence should be:
explanation: No answer description is available.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: S 1 : Minnie went shopping one morning. P : Disappointed She turned around and returned to the parking lot. Q : She got out and walked to the nearest shop. R : She drove her car into the parking lot and stopped. S : It was there that she realised that she'd forgotten her purse at home. S 6 : She drove home with an empty basket. The Proper sequence should be:
explanation: No answer description is available.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: S 1 : Far away in a little street there is a poor house. P : Her face is thin and worn and her hands are coarse, pricked by a needle, for she is a seam stress. Q : One of the windows is open and through it I can see a poor woman. R : He has a fever and asking for oranges. S : In a bed in a corner of the room her little boy is lying ill. S 6 : His mother has nothing to give but water, so he is crying. The Proper sequence should be:
explanation: No answer description is available.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: S 1 : A noise started above their heads. P : But people did not take it seriously. Q : That was to show everyone that there was something wrong R : It was a dangerous thing to do. S : For, within minutes the ship began to sink. S 6 : Nearly 200 lives were lost on the fateful day. The Proper sequence should be:
explanation: S1 introduces a noise. Q explains its purpose as a warning. P describes the lack of serious reaction, which R states was dangerous. S then explains why it was dangerous, leading to the conclusion in S6.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: S 1 : American private lies may seem shallow. P : Students would walk away with books they had not paid for. Q : A Chinese journalist commented on a curious institution: the library R : Their public morality, however, impressed visitors. S : But in general they returned them. S 6 : This would not happen in china, he said. The Proper sequence should be:
explanation: S1 discusses American private lives. R contrasts this with their public morality, which impressed visitors. Q introduces a specific example of this public morality through a Chinese journalist's observation about libraries. P and S describe the specific behavior of students with library books, illustrating the public morality.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: S 1 : The path of Venus lies inside the path of the Earth. P : When at its farthest from the Earth, Venus is 160 million away Q : With such a wide range between its greatest and leat distances it is natural that at sometimes Venus appears much brighter than at others. R : No other body ever comes so near the Earth, with the exception of the Moon and occasional comet or asteroid. S : When Venus is at its nearest to the earth it is only 26 million miles away. S 6 : When at its brightest, it is easily seen with the naked eye in broad daylight. The Proper sequence should be:
explanation: S1 introduces Venus's path. S states its nearest distance, which R emphasizes as uniquely close. P then provides its farthest distance, and Q summarizes these two extremes, linking them to variations in brightness.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: S 1 : In India marriages are usually arranged by parents P : Sometimes boys and girls do not like th idea of arranged marriages. Q : Most young people accepts the state of affairs. R : Shanta was like that. S : They assume their parents can make good choices. S 6 : She felt she was a modern girl and not subject for bargaining. The Proper sequence should be:
explanation: S1 introduces arranged marriages. Q states that most young people accept this, and S explains why. P then introduces the contrasting idea that some dislike it, and R provides Shanta as an example of this.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: S 1 : She said on the phone that she would report for duty next day. P : We waited for few days then we decided to go to her place. Q : But she did not. R : we found it locked. S : Even after that we waited for her quite a few days. S 6 : Eventually we reported to the police. The Proper sequence should be:
explanation: S1 states a promise. Q immediately contradicts it. P describes the initial response to her absence, followed by R, the discovery at her place. S indicates further waiting, leading to the final action in S6.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: S 1 : Several sub-cities have been planned around capital. P : Dwarga is the first among them. Q : They are expected to alleviate the problem of housing. R : It is coming up in the south west of capital. S : It will cater to over one million people when completed. S 6 : Hopefully the housing problem will not be as acute at present after these sub-cities are built. The Proper sequence should be:
explanation: S1 introduces the planning of sub-cities. Q states their purpose. P introduces Dwarga as an example, with R describing its location and S its capacity. S6 then concludes by reiterating the expected outcome related to housing.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: S 1 : Forecasting the weather has always been a difficult business. P : During a period of drought, streams and rivers dried up, the cattle died from thirst and were ruined. Q : Many different things affect the weather and we have to study them carefully to make accurate forecast. R : Ancient Egyptians had no need of weather in the Nile Valley hardly ever changes. S : In early times, when there were no instruments, such as thermometer or the barometer, man looked for tell-tale signs in the sky. S 6 : He made his forecasts by watching flights of the birds or the way smoke rose from fire. The Proper sequence should be:
explanation: S1 introduces the difficulty of weather forecasting. Q explains this difficulty by noting the many factors involved. P provides an example of severe weather impact, highlighting the importance of accurate forecasts. R then offers a contrast, mentioning the ancient Egyptians who had stable weather. S shifts to historical methods of forecasting before modern instruments.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: S 1 : As he passed beneath her he heard the swish of her wings. P : He was not falling head long now. Q : The monstrous terror seized him. R : But it only lasted a minute. S : He could hear nothing. S 6 : The next moment he felt his wings spread outwards. The Proper sequence should be:
explanation: S1 describes an event. Q immediately follows as the emotional reaction. S describes a sensory effect of this terror. R then states that this terror was brief. P describes a physical change that occurs after the terror, which is then explained by S6.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: S 1 : Ms. Parasuram started a Petrol Pump in Madras. P : A total to 12 girls now work at the pump. Q : She advertised in newspapers for women staff. R : They operate in 2 shifts. S : The response was good. S 6 : Thus she has shown the way for many others. The Proper sequence should be:
explanation: S1 introduces the petrol pump. Q describes the action taken to hire staff. S states the positive outcome of this action. P gives the number of staff hired, and R provides further details about their work.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: S 1 : Sun birds are among the smallest of Indian birds. P : Though they are functionally similar to the humming birds of the New World, they are totally unrelated. Q : They do eat insects too. R : They are also some of the most brilliantly coloured birds. S : sun birds feed on nectar mostly and helped in pollination. S 6 : Our common sun birds are the purple sun bird, the glossy black species and purple rumped sun bird, the yellow and maroon species. The Proper sequence should be:
explanation: S1 introduces sun birds as small. R adds another general characteristic, that they are brilliantly coloured. P then introduces their functional similarity to hummingbirds, which S elaborates on by describing their nectar-feeding and pollination role. Q adds that they also eat insects.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: S 1 : For decades, American society has been calling a melting pot P : Differences remained - in appearence, mannerisms, customs, speech, religion and more. Q : The term has long been a cliche and half-truth. R : But homogenisation was never acheived. S : Yes, immigrants from diverse cultures and traditions did cast off vestiges of their native lands and become almost imperceptibly woven in to the American fabric. S 6 : In recent years, such differences accentuated by the arrival of immigrants from Asia and other parts of the world in the United States - have become something to celebrate and to nurture. The Proper sequence should be:
explanation: S1 introduces the concept of American society as a melting pot. S then affirms the partial truth of this idea, describing how immigrants integrated. Q then provides an overall assessment, calling the term a 'cliche and half-truth,' with S representing the 'truth' part. R introduces the 'half' part by stating that homogenization was never fully achieved, and P elaborates on the specific differences that remained.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: S 1 : I took cigarettes from my case. P : But when the fit of coughing was over, he replaced it between his lips. Q : I lit one of them and placed it between the lips. R : Then with a feeble hand he removed the cigarette. S : Slowly he took a pull at it and coughed violently. S 6 : Then he continues to draw on it. The Proper sequence should be:
explanation: S1 describes taking cigarettes. Q follows with the action of lighting and placing one. S describes the other person's immediate reaction (pulling and coughing). R details the removal of the cigarette, and P describes its replacement after the coughing fit.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: S 1 : There is difference between Gandhiji's concept of secularism and that of Nehru's. P : Nehru's idea of secularism was equal indifference to all religions and bothering about none of them. Q : According to Gandhiji, all religions are equally true and each scripture is worthy of respect. R : Such secularism which means the rejection of all religions is contrary to our culture and tradition. S : In Gandhiji's view, secularism stands for equal respect for all religions. S 6 : Instead of doing any good, such secularism can do harm instead of good. The Proper sequence should be:
explanation: S1 introduces the contrast between Gandhiji's and Nehru's secularism. S and Q together define Gandhiji's concept of equal respect for all religions. P then introduces Nehru's contrasting idea of indifference. R comments on Nehru's concept, interpreting it as a rejection of religions.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: S 1 : As a dramatist Rabindranath was not what might be called a success. P : His dramas were moulded on the lines of the traditional Indian village dramas than the dramas of modern world. Q : His plays were more a catalogue of ideas than a vehicle of the expression of action. R : Actually the drama has always been the life of Indian people, as it deals with legends of gods and goddesses. S : Although in his short stories and novels he was able to create living and well defined characters, he did not seem to be able to do so in dramas S 6 : Therefor, drama forms the essential part of the traditional Indian Culture. The Proper sequence should be:
explanation: S1 states Tagore's lack of success as a dramatist. Q explains this by noting his plays prioritized ideas over action. S further clarifies this by contrasting his character development in novels versus dramas. P then explains that his dramas were based on traditional Indian village forms, which R provides context for, describing the nature of traditional Indian drama.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Rohan and Rohit are twin brothers, but they do not look ......
explanation: The phrase 'look alike' is an idiom used to describe people who resemble each other. Since Rohan and Rohit are twins but do not resemble each other, 'alike' is the appropriate word.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: To err is ...... to forgive divine.
explanation: This is a well-known proverb, 'To err is human, to forgive divine,' meaning that making mistakes is a natural part of human nature, while forgiveness is a noble, god-like quality.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The ruling party will have to put its own house ...... order.
explanation: The idiom 'to put one's house in order' means to organize one's affairs, resolve internal problems, or improve one's own situation. Therefore, 'in' is the correct preposition.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: ...... of old paintings is a job for experts.
explanation: 'Restoration' refers to the process of repairing and preserving old or damaged items, such as paintings, to return them to their original or near-original condition. This task requires specialized expertise.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: During Diwali the shops are ...... of people.
explanation: The phrase 'full of people' is the most natural and grammatically correct way to describe shops being packed with customers during a busy period like Diwali. While 'crowded' is also relevant, 'crowded of people' is not idiomatic.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The paths of glory lead ...... to the grave.
explanation: This is a famous line from Thomas Gray's 'Elegy Written in a Country Churchyard.' In this context, 'but' means 'only' or 'merely,' implying that all achievements and fame ultimately lead to death.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The telephone ...... several times before I answered it.
explanation: The past perfect tense ('had rung') is used here to indicate an action (the telephone ringing) that was completed before another past action (I answered it). This clearly establishes the sequence of events.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: He passed the examination in the first class because he ......
explanation: The past perfect tense ('had worked hard') is appropriate here because the action of working hard occurred and was completed *before* the action of passing the examination (which is in the simple past). This establishes a clear cause-and-effect relationship in the past.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Jawaharlal spent his childhood ...... Anand Bhawan.
explanation: 'At' is the most appropriate preposition for indicating a specific location or residence where someone spent time. Anand Bhawan is a particular place, making 'at' the correct choice.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: If negotiations are to prove fruitful, there must not only be sincerity on each side, but there must also be ...... in the sincerity of the other side.
explanation: The phrase 'faith in the sincerity' is the most idiomatic and appropriate choice. It implies trust and confidence in the other party's honest intentions, which is crucial for successful negotiations.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: I hate sitting ...... him as he always smells of garlic.
explanation: 'Beside' means 'next to' or 'at the side of,' which is the correct preposition to indicate sitting in close proximity to someone. 'Besides' means 'in addition to' or 'apart from,' which is incorrect in this context.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Some regions of our country still remain ...... to the average man.
explanation: Inaccessible means unable to be reached or entered, which accurately describes regions that the average person cannot get to.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: It ...... that Prashant will not be selected for the post
explanation: It seems that is the correct idiomatic expression used to convey an impression or a likelihood based on available information.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: In Bush, Saddam was up ...... more than his match.
explanation: The phrase "up against" means to be confronted with or opposed by someone or something, indicating Saddam was facing a formidable opponent in Bush.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: I haven't seen you ...... a week.
explanation: For is used to indicate a duration of time, making "for a week" the correct choice to express the period over which the person has not been seen.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: I listened, but I had no idea what he was ...... about.
explanation: Talking about is the correct idiomatic phrase used to refer to the subject or topic of someone's speech.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The car in which the minister was traveling ...... with an accident.
explanation: The idiomatic expression "met with an accident" is the correct way to state that someone or something was involved in an accident.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The non cooperative attitude of the members can only ...... the image of the society.
explanation: A non-cooperative attitude would negatively impact the society's reputation, and "spoil" accurately describes damaging or ruining its image.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Sonika is quite intelligent but rather ......
explanation: The conjunction "but" indicates a contrast, so "lazy" provides a negative trait that contrasts with her intelligence.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: In a little-publicised deal, Pepsi, Cola has ...... the entire soft drink market in Afghanistan.
explanation: Captured is commonly used in business to describe successfully gaining control or a significant share of a market.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: He ...... in wearing the old fashioned coat in spite of his wife's disapproval.
explanation: Persists in means to continue an action or behavior despite opposition or difficulty, which accurately describes wearing the coat despite disapproval.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: She ...... a brief appearance at the end of party.
explanation: The idiomatic phrase "put in an appearance" means to attend an event for a short period of time.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Life is to death as pleasure is to ......
explanation: This is an analogy where the relationship is antonymous; "pain" is the direct opposite of "pleasure," just as "death" is the opposite of "life."

subject: Verbal Ability
text: This, partly, explains how the Mehta family has been able to ...... its lavish lifestyle in recent times, despite the fact that all its assets have been ......
explanation: Keep up means to maintain a certain standard or pace, which fits maintaining a lavish lifestyle. Destroyed assets would make this difficult, thus fitting the "despite" clause.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The machine is difficult to build ...... easy to maintain.
explanation: The conjunction "but" is used to introduce a contrasting idea, indicating that despite being difficult to build, the machine is easy to maintain.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: If you work beyond your capacity, you will naturally feel ......
explanation: Working beyond one's capacity naturally leads to physical and mental exhaustion, making "tired" the most fitting consequence.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: If you persists in telling lies to me I shall sue you ...... slander.
explanation: The correct preposition to use when suing someone for a specific offense is "for," as in "sue for slander."

subject: Verbal Ability
text: His father-in-law ...... him up in business.
explanation: The idiomatic phrase "set him up in business" means to provide someone with the necessary support or capital to start their own business.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: ...... works of reference are valuable as Encyclopedia, Brittanica.
explanation: Few (without "a") implies a scarcity or a small number, suggesting that not many works of reference are as valuable as Encyclopedia Britannica.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: This book is quite similar ......
options: of that film we saw at school
to the one I read last week
than a story told by our teacher
explanation: The correct preposition to use with the adjective "similar" is "to," making "similar to the one I read last week" the grammatically correct phrase.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: If our friends are not able to take us in their car, we must make ...... arrangements to go to the airport.
explanation: Alternative arrangements refers to other available options or plans when the primary one is not feasible.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: A crescendo of metallic thuds arose from the market, where the iron-smiths were ...... the pieces of metals.
explanation: Iron-smiths typically use hammers to shape metal, and the sound of "metallic thuds" is characteristic of the action of "hammering."

subject: Verbal Ability
text: I haven't eaten an apple ...... a long while.
explanation: For is used to specify a duration of time, making "for a long while" the correct phrase to indicate the period over which no apple has been eaten.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The parliament invested the new organisation ...... judicial authority.
explanation: The verb "invest" when granting power or authority is correctly followed by the preposition "with," meaning to endow or furnish with.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Owing to the power cut in the area, factories are being forced to ...... men
explanation: Lay off is the correct phrasal verb meaning to dismiss employees, often temporarily, due to economic reasons or lack of work, which aligns with the impact of a power cut on factories.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Man must ...... to stop pollution.
explanation: Act means to take action or initiate steps, which is the appropriate verb for addressing and stopping pollution.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: He was sent to the prison for his ......
explanation: One is sent to prison for committing a 'crime,' which is an action or omission that constitutes an offense punishable by law. 'Sin' and 'vice' are moral or religious transgressions, while 'guilt' is the state of having committed an offense.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The answer was written ...... blue ink.
explanation: When referring to the medium used for writing, such as ink or pencil, the preposition 'in' is conventionally used. Therefore, 'written in blue ink' is the correct phrasing.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Many ...... decisions were taken at the meeting.
explanation: 'Historic' refers to something famous or important in history, or likely to be so, fitting the context of significant decisions. 'Historical' relates to history or past events in general, which is less precise here.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Those who persist in the endeavor at long last triumph ...... the odds of life.
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is 'triumph over,' which means to overcome or defeat something, such as difficulties or challenges. This idiom perfectly fits the context of overcoming the 'odds of life'.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: In the modern materialistic society, the only aim of people appears to be ...... money by fair means or foul.
explanation: 'Acquire' means to gain possession of something, which accurately describes the general aim of obtaining money. The phrase 'by fair means or foul' further supports 'acquire' as it covers all methods of obtaining wealth.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: What is the ...... for an air letter?
explanation: 'Postage' refers to the amount charged for sending a letter or parcel through the postal service. 'Stamp' is the physical item used to pay for postage, but the question asks for the charge itself.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: He became enamored ...... her grace when he first saw her dance.
explanation: The correct preposition to use with the adjective 'enamored' is 'of.' To be 'enamored of' someone or something means to be captivated or filled with love for them.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Rama was so badly injured that he needed ...... care in the hospital.
explanation: 'Intensive care' is a standard medical term referring to specialized treatment for critically ill or injured patients. The other options do not form a recognized medical phrase in this context.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: You cannot devise a method which ...... all possibility of errors.
explanation: 'Excludes' means to prevent something from being included or considered, which is the most precise word for a method designed to eliminate all possibility of errors. 'Avoids' is similar but 'excludes all possibility' is a stronger, more definitive phrase.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Many areas of the city were ...... into darkness for several hours.
explanation: The phrase 'plunged into darkness' is a common idiom used to describe a sudden and complete loss of light, often due to a power failure. The other options do not fit this idiomatic expression.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Find the ...... that accompany these cartoons.
explanation: 'Captions' are the short pieces of text that accompany and explain illustrations, photographs, or cartoons. They provide context or dialogue for the visual content.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: I don't really know how to ...... the problem.
explanation: To 'tackle a problem' means to deal with it or make an effort to solve it. While one can 'cope with' a problem, 'cope' requires the preposition 'with' and doesn't fit the sentence structure as well as 'tackle'.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The English schemed to continue their rule in India by playing off one community ...... the other.
explanation: The idiom 'to play off one against the other' means to exploit the differences or rivalries between two parties for one's own benefit. This perfectly describes the strategy mentioned in the sentence.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The passengers were afraid, but the captain ...... them that there was no danger.
explanation: To 'assure' someone means to tell them something confidently to remove their doubts or fears. This is the most appropriate action for a captain to take when passengers are afraid.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The family gave father a gold watch on the ...... of his fiftieth birthday.
explanation: The phrase 'on the occasion of' is a standard idiom used to refer to a particular event or celebration. It fits perfectly when marking a significant milestone like a birthday.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The park ...... as far as the river.
explanation: 'Extends' is the most appropriate verb to describe the physical reach or boundary of an area, indicating that the park stretches out to the river. The other options do not convey this sense of spatial reach.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Many of the advances of civilisation have been conceived by young people just on the ...... of adulthood
explanation: The idiom 'on the threshold of' means at the point of beginning something new or entering a new stage. 'Threshold of adulthood' is a common phrase referring to the cusp of becoming an adult.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Arti pulled a long ...... when she was told that she could not go to Agra.
explanation: To 'pull a long face' is an idiom meaning to look sad, disappointed, or displeased. This expression perfectly describes Arti's reaction to the news.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Brothers must live in harmony. They must never fall ......
explanation: The phrasal verb 'fall out' (with someone) means to have an argument and stop being friendly. This is the appropriate choice to contrast with 'live in harmony'.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: I was astounded at his ...... lack of knowledge about the Continent of Africa.
explanation: 'Colossal' means extremely large or great, conveying a sense of immense scale. When paired with 'lack of knowledge,' it emphasizes the astonishing and surprising extent of the deficiency, fitting the feeling of being 'astounded'.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Ayesha always ...... the permission of her father before going for movies.
explanation: The word 'always' indicates a habitual action, requiring the simple present tense. For a third-person singular subject like 'Ayesha,' the correct verb form is 'seeks'.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: He felt no ...... as he plunged the knife into her back.
explanation: 'Qualms' refers to uneasy feelings of doubt, worry, or misgiving about one's own conduct or the morality of an action. To feel 'no qualms' means to have no hesitation or moral reservations, which fits the context of a ruthless act.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: You haven't had your lunch yet, ...... you?
explanation: This is a tag question. When the main clause is negative ('haven't had'), the tag question must be positive. The auxiliary verb from the main clause ('have') is used in the tag, making 'have you?' the correct choice.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The manner in which bombs exploded in five trains with in a short span of time suggests that it is a part of a ......
explanation: A 'conspiracy' refers to a secret plan by a group of people to do something unlawful or harmful. The coordinated nature of multiple bomb explosions strongly suggests a planned, secretive effort by a group.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Wheat ...... carbohydrates, vitamins, proteins, and dietary fibre in our daily diet.
explanation: 'Provides' means to make something available or supply it. In the context of nutrition, wheat 'provides' essential nutrients like carbohydrates, vitamins, proteins, and dietary fibre to our diet.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The waiter hasn't brought the coffee ...... I've been here an hour already.
explanation: "Yet" is used in negative sentences to indicate that something expected has not happened up to the present time.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: In our zeal for progress we should not ...... executive with more power.
explanation: To "arm someone with power" means to provide them with the necessary authority or means to act, which fits the context of giving an executive more power.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: You've only three months to complete the course, Don't give ...... now.
explanation: The phrasal verb "give up" means to stop trying or surrender, which is appropriate advice when facing a deadline for a course.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The traveler slept under the ...... shade of banyan tree.
explanation: "Cool shade" is a common and idiomatic phrase used to describe a pleasant, refreshing temperature found under a tree.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: She has an aversion ...... taking even onion and garlic.
explanation: The correct preposition to use with the noun "aversion" is "to," forming the phrase "aversion to something" which means a strong dislike or disinclination towards it.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The river overflowed its ...... and flooded the area.
explanation: Rivers have "banks," which are the land alongside the water. When a river floods, it overflows these banks.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: ...... The arrival of the police, nobody went near the victim.
explanation: "Till" (or "until") is used to indicate the point in time when an action or state ceases or changes, meaning nobody approached the victim before the police arrived.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: When I was a child, I ......to school everyday instead going by cycle.
explanation: The phrase "When I was a child" indicates a past habit or repeated action, for which the simple past tense ("walked") is the correct grammatical choice.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Farida sings very well and ...... does salim.
explanation: The structure "so + auxiliary verb + subject" is used to express that a second person or thing does the same as the first, meaning Salim also sings very well.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The old Nature versus ...... debate regarding crime continues even today.
explanation: The well-known psychological and sociological debate is "Nature versus Nurture," which examines the relative importance of genetics (nature) and environmental factors (nurture) in shaping an individual's traits and behaviors.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The Sun ...... at six this morning.
explanation: "Rose" is the simple past tense of the intransitive verb "rise," which correctly describes the sun moving upwards in the sky.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: That rule is applicable ...... every one.
explanation: The correct preposition to use with "applicable" is "to," forming the phrase "applicable to someone/something" to indicate relevance or suitability.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Her parents will never give their ...... to so much an unsuitable match.
explanation: "Give one's consent to" is an idiomatic phrase meaning to grant permission or approval, which is appropriate in the context of parents approving a marriage match.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: He tried to ...... himself against a horde of ruffians.
explanation: To "defend oneself against" means to protect oneself from an attack or harm, which is the most suitable action when confronted by a group of ruffians.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: According to corporate circles data is pushing through the merger to create financially ...... company in the processed foods business, the group's thrust area for the 1990's
explanation: A merger is typically undertaken to create a stronger, more dominant entity. "Financially powerful" best describes a company with significant resources and influence in its market.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Indeed, all over the world, more and more people are ...... coffee.
explanation: The most direct and common verb to describe the consumption of coffee is "drinking," indicating that more people are consuming it worldwide.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Like any other country India has its ...... share of superstitions.
explanation: "Fair share" is an idiom meaning a reasonable or considerable amount, implying that India has its expected portion of superstitions, similar to other countries.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The new education policy provides a useful ...... for the planners to remove illiteracy.
explanation: A "breakthrough" refers to a significant and often sudden advance or discovery that helps overcome a problem, making it suitable for a policy aimed at removing illiteracy.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: People who ...... on horses usually lose in the end.
explanation: To "gamble on horses" is the correct idiomatic expression for betting money on the outcome of horse races.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: About twenty clerks were made ...... when the banks introduced computers.
explanation: When jobs are eliminated due to automation or technological advancements, the employees whose roles are no longer needed are said to be made "redundant."

subject: Verbal Ability
text: After the rain the weather ...... and the sun came out.
explanation: The phrasal verb "clear up" is used to describe the weather becoming brighter and less cloudy, typically after rain or a storm.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The building comprises ...... sixty rooms.
explanation: The verb "comprise" is transitive and means "to consist of" or "to include," therefore it does not require the preposition "of" when listing its components.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Namrata was found to ...... the required qualifications for the job.
explanation: To "possess" qualifications means to have or own them, which is the appropriate verb to describe someone having the necessary skills or credentials for a job.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Government buildings are ...... on the Republic day.
explanation: "Illuminated" means to light up something, often for decorative or ceremonial purposes, which is a common practice for government buildings on special occasions like Republic Day.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: we had ...... wonderful time at the party last night.
explanation: The phrase "such a" is used before an adjective and a singular countable noun to emphasize the quality, making "such a wonderful time" the correct and idiomatic choice.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: An employment advertisement should ...... the number of vacancies.
explanation: To "specify" means to state clearly and in detail. An employment advertisement should clearly and precisely state the exact number of vacancies available.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: When varun left the cocktail party he was as ...... as a judge.
explanation: The common idiom "as sober as a judge" means completely sober, not under the influence of alcohol. This idiom is used here to imply Varun was not drunk despite being at a cocktail party.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The criminal seems to have acted in ...... the three others.
explanation: "Collusion" refers to secret cooperation or conspiracy, often for an illegal or deceitful purpose. This word accurately describes criminals acting together with others.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Soft minded individuals are ...... to embrace all kinds superstitions.
explanation: "Prone to" means having a tendency or likelihood to do or experience something, often something undesirable. Soft-minded individuals are easily influenced and thus more likely to believe in superstitions.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The stenographer is very efficient. He is ...... to his firm.
explanation: An "asset" is a valuable person or thing. An efficient stenographer is a valuable resource and contributes positively to the firm's operations.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: However, the group's long-term strategy is to ...... on core sector business connected with infrastructure and energy.
explanation: To "concentrate on" something means to focus all attention or effort on it. The group's strategy is to focus its efforts specifically on core sector businesses related to infrastructure and energy.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The man came in a van to ...... the television set.
explanation: To "mend" something means to repair it, especially something that is broken or damaged. A technician would come to mend or fix a broken television set.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Nobody can ...... me to do anything which I do not want to do.
explanation: To "compel" someone means to force or oblige them to do something. The sentence implies that the speaker cannot be forced into actions they do not wish to perform.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: A sanguine outlook is associated with the ......
explanation: A "sanguine outlook" describes a hopeful and confident perspective. An "optimist" is a person who typically holds such a positive view of the future.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: His conduct is bad, and his honesty is not ...... suspicion.
explanation: The phrase "not above suspicion" means that someone's honesty or integrity is questionable and they are indeed suspected of wrongdoing. Given his bad conduct, his honesty is certainly not beyond question.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: It is 14 years since i ...... him.
explanation: The correct grammatical structure for expressing a duration since a past event is "It is [time period] since [simple past tense]". Therefore, "saw" (simple past of see) is the appropriate verb form here.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: It being an ...... issue, it is not correct to introduce questions of morality in to the debate.
explanation: An "amoral" issue is one that is not concerned with or does not have moral implications. Therefore, it would be inappropriate to introduce questions of morality into a debate about such an issue.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Natwarlal ...... them all for a ride by producing false documents.
explanation: The idiom "to take someone for a ride" means to deceive, trick, or swindle them. Natwarlal used false documents to trick others.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: True brevity ...... in saying only what needs to be said.
explanation: The phrase "consists in" means to have as its main or essential feature. True brevity is essentially defined by the act of saying only what is necessary.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Health is too important to be ......
explanation: Health is a vital aspect of life that requires proper care and attention. It is too important to be "neglected," meaning to be ignored or not given proper care.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: In hot weather I like lying in the ...... of a tree.
explanation: "Shade" refers to the coolness and reduced light found under a tree, providing relief from hot weather. "Shadow" is merely the dark outline, not necessarily implying coolness.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: It was indeed unreasonable ...... him to leave this job and start business.
explanation: The correct idiomatic expression is "It is [adjective] of [someone] to do something," which attributes the quality (in this case, unreasonableness) to the person's action.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: One major ...... between the Election Commission and the Union Government related to the powers of the former in respect of the deployment of central police forces at places where are elections is held.
explanation: A "conflict" refers to a serious disagreement or dispute, which accurately describes a major issue concerning powers between two significant bodies like the Election Commission and the Union Government.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: We shall not to be able to use your ability in court unless we can find someone to ...... to statements.
explanation: To "corroborate" means to confirm or give support to a statement or theory. In a legal context, it means to provide evidence that supports the truth of a witness's statements.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: He is too dull ...... this problem.
explanation: The grammatical construction "too [adjective] to [verb]" is used to express that someone or something possesses a quality to an excessive degree, preventing a particular action. Here, he is too dull to be able to solve the problem.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: He is the person who is ...... to blame.
explanation: The phrase "most to blame" is an idiomatic expression used to indicate the person who bears the greatest responsibility or fault for a negative situation.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: We had to pay more taxi fare because the driver brought us by a ...... route.
explanation: A "circuitous" route is one that is longer and less direct than necessary, often winding or indirect, which would naturally result in a higher taxi fare.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: He was accused ...... stealing his aunt's necklace.
explanation: The correct preposition to use with the verb "accused" when specifying the charge or offense is "of." One is "accused of" a crime.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: He knew everything better than anybody else, and it was an affront to his ...... vanity that you should disagree with him.
explanation: "Overweening" means showing excessive confidence or pride. "Overweening vanity" perfectly describes an inflated sense of self-importance that would be offended by disagreement.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: It's very kind of you to ...... to speak at the meeting.
explanation: To "agree to" do something means to consent or give permission to perform an action. In this context, it means consenting to speak at the meeting.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Pakistan lost a ...... wicket just when they seemed to be doing so well, and that led to their eventual defeat.
explanation: “Crucial” means extremely important or decisive, fitting the context of a wicket that significantly impacted the game's outcome and led to defeat.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Sita's heart ...... at the sight of the beautiful diamond necklace.
explanation: “Heart leapt” is an idiom used to describe a sudden feeling of joy, excitement, or surprise, which is appropriate when seeing something beautiful and desirable like a diamond necklace.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: He lives near a lovely ...... of countryside.
explanation: “Stretch of countryside” is a common collocation used to describe an expanse or area of land, fitting the context of living near a lovely rural area.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Ambition is one of those ......which are never satisfied.
explanation: Ambition is a strong desire or drive, making “passions” the most suitable word to describe something that is never satisfied, as passions often drive individuals relentlessly.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: He is a person of sound character and ...... disposition.
explanation: “Amiable” describes a person's friendly and pleasant disposition, which complements “sound character” well, indicating a person who is both morally upright and pleasant to be around.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: If I take a state roadways bus, I'll get late, ...... ?
explanation: The main clause “I'll get late” uses the auxiliary verb “will” and the subject “I”. For a positive statement, the tag question uses the negative form of the auxiliary verb and the same subject, hence “won't I”.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: In high school many of us never realised the importance that grammar would ...... in later life.
explanation: The phrase “play importance” or “play a role/part” is a common idiom meaning to have an effect or influence, indicating the significance grammar would have later in life.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: In a changing and ...... unstructured business environment, creativity and innovation are being ...... demanded of executives.
explanation: “Progressively unstructured” indicates an environment that is continuously becoming less structured, while “increasingly demanded” signifies a growing need for creativity and innovation in such a dynamic context.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: On account of the dearth of grass on the arid plains the cattle became ......
explanation: “Emaciated” means abnormally thin or weak due to a lack of food, which is the direct consequence for cattle experiencing a “dearth of grass” on “arid plains.”

subject: Verbal Ability
text: A woman came in with a baby who, she said, ...... a safety pin.
explanation: The action of swallowing the safety pin occurred before the woman “came in” and “said” it. The past perfect tense (“had just swallowed”) is used to describe an action completed immediately before another past event.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The Hubble Space Telescope will search for planets around the stars, a key to the ...... extraterrestrial life, and examine interstellar dust and gases out of which stars are born.
explanation: “Quest” refers to a long or arduous search for something, which accurately describes the ongoing and challenging endeavor of searching for extraterrestrial life.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: She ...... Rs 80 out of the bank every Friday.
explanation: “Draw money” is the correct idiom used to describe the act of withdrawing funds from a bank account.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The American ...... presented his credentials to the President of India.
explanation: An ambassador is the highest-ranking diplomatic representative of one country to another, and presenting credentials to the head of state is a formal duty performed upon assuming their post.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Once he has signed the agreement, he won't be able to ......
explanation: To “back out” means to withdraw from an agreement or commitment, which is the appropriate action one cannot take after signing a formal agreement.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: It was difficult to remove my feet as it had got stuck ...... in the mud.
explanation: “Firmly” means securely or tightly, which accurately describes how feet would be stuck in mud if they were difficult to remove.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: He is a very careful person, he never takes side but remains ......
explanation: “Impartial” means treating all rivals or disputants equally and fairly, which perfectly describes a person who “never takes side” and is careful in their judgments.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: West Bengal ...... plentiful rainfall and is consequently a very green part of the country.
explanation: West Bengal “receives” plentiful rainfall, meaning it gets a large amount of rain, which directly explains why it is a very green part of the country.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: this brand of TV is quite inferior ...... that one.
explanation: The adjective “inferior” is always followed by the preposition “to” when making a comparison between two things.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: It is not what you say that ...... but what you do
explanation: The phrase “what you do that matters” is a common expression emphasizing that actions are more important than words. “Matters” means to be of importance or significance.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: 'Please' and 'Thank you' are the little courtesies by which we keep the ...... of life oiled and running smoothly.
explanation: This is a metaphor where courtesies are compared to oil, which keeps a “machine” running smoothly, implying that politeness helps maintain the harmonious functioning of daily life.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: I put ...... the light and slept.
explanation: The phrasal verb “put out” means to extinguish a light or fire, which is the appropriate action before going to sleep.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: ...... all intents and purposes, the manager is the master of the firm.
explanation: “To all intents and purposes” is a fixed idiom meaning “in every practical sense” or “virtually,” indicating that the manager is effectively the master of the firm.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The pilot had been warned about the storm, before he ......
explanation: The phrasal verb “took off” means for an aircraft to depart from the ground, which is the relevant action a pilot would perform before encountering a storm.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Hardly had he arrived ...... it started raining.
explanation: The correlative conjunction used with “Hardly had” is “when,” forming the structure “Hardly had... when...” to indicate that one event immediately followed another.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: I ...... a car to be absolutely necessary these days.
explanation: “Consider” is the correct verb that fits the grammatical structure “consider + object + to be + adjective/noun phrase,” meaning to view or deem something in a particular way.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Besides other provisions, that shopkeeper deals ...... cosmetics.
explanation: To "deal in" something means to buy and sell a particular product or commodity, which is appropriate for a shopkeeper and cosmetics.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Leave a two inch ...... on each page for the teacher's remarks.
explanation: A "margin" refers to the blank space around the edges of a page, typically left for notes or remarks.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The team was well trained and strong, but somehow their ...... was low.
explanation: "Morale" refers to the confidence, enthusiasm, and discipline of a person or group at a particular time, which can be low or high.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The lovers were meeting each other secretly, but their ...... affair was soon known to everyone.
explanation: "Clandestine" means kept secret or done secretively, often implying something illicit or improper, which perfectly describes a secret affair that becomes known.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The king ...... the rebel.
explanation: To "pardon" someone means to officially release them from punishment for an offense, which is the appropriate term for a king dealing with a rebel.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: I shall call ...... you tomorrow.
explanation: The phrasal verb "call on" means to pay a visit to a person. "Call at" is used for visiting a place.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: She ...... from the crowd because of her height and flaming red hair.
explanation: The phrasal verb "stood out" means to be easily noticeable or distinct from a group, which is appropriate given her unique features.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: In a large cities people are cut ...... from nature.
explanation: The phrasal verb "cut off from" means to be isolated or separated from something, which accurately describes people's relationship with nature in large cities.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The more your action and thought are allied and ...... the happier you grow.
explanation: "Integrated" means to combine two or more things so that they work together effectively, implying harmony between action and thought.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: We had a ...... of warm weather in February.
explanation: A "spell of weather" is a common idiom referring to a short period of a particular type of weather.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Physically we are now all neighbors, but psychologically. we are ...... to each other.
explanation: The sentence contrasts physical proximity with psychological distance, making "strangers" the appropriate word to describe a lack of deep connection or understanding.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: When their examinations are over, the children gleefully ...... the books they had been reading.
explanation: To "abandon" the books means to stop using or dealing with them, which children would gleefully do after their examinations are over.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Chintu is ...... small to start playing cricket now.
explanation: The construction "too...to" is used to express that something is excessively a certain way, preventing a subsequent action, e.g., "too small to start playing."

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The thief ...... all the money.
explanation: The phrasal verb "made off with" means to steal something and escape with it, which accurately describes the action of a thief.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The luggage was ...... heavy for him to lift
explanation: The construction "too...to" is used to express that something is excessively a certain way, preventing a subsequent action, e.g., "too heavy for him to lift."

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Walking at 3'o clock, I heard the ...... of thunder.
explanation: "Rumble" is the specific and characteristic deep, resonant sound associated with thunder.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: A stone that goes on rolling ...... no moss.
explanation: This is a common proverb, "A rolling stone gathers no moss," meaning that a person who is always moving or changing jobs will not accumulate wealth or responsibilities.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The earth is at present in great danger of becoming uninhabitable because of ...... environmental pollution which is going on at an incredible rapid pace.
explanation: "Colossal" means extremely large or great in size or degree, effectively conveying the immense scale and severity of environmental pollution.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Moreover, a fact finding mission ...... by BSN to India in January this year strongly recommended that the French group should go it alone, and not hand over ...... to an Indian Partner.
explanation: A mission is "dispatched" (sent out for a specific purpose), and in a business context, a group would "hand over control" rather than authority or power to a partner.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: There is a keen ...... in each trade.
explanation: "Competition" refers to the rivalry between businesses or individuals striving for the same objective, which is a fundamental aspect of trade.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Now, the management graduate can expect to have a prosperous life on a ...... income without having to depend on finding a place in family busiess having to tend the paternal estates.
explanation: A "regular income" refers to a steady and consistent salary or earnings, which allows a management graduate to lead a prosperous life independently.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The boy fell ...... bicycle.
explanation: The phrasal verb "fell off" means to become separated from something by falling, which is the correct usage when describing someone falling from a bicycle.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The French ...... reputed to have a very good sense of humour.
explanation: When "the French" refers to the people of France collectively, it is treated as a plural noun, requiring the plural verb "are."

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Johny, where are you? ...... up this tree.
explanation: "Here I am" is the standard and most natural way to announce one's presence in response to a question about location.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: That the poor in our country, are happy is
explanation: An "illusion" is a false idea or belief, or a deceptive appearance, which accurately describes the notion that the poor in a country are happy despite their circumstances.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The robbers were arrested and ...... prison yesterday.
explanation: Taken to prison is the correct idiomatic expression for being transported to a correctional facility by authorities.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The cinema ...... a welcome escape from cramped and dull city life and the Indians are avid movie-goers.
explanation: The verb 'offers' is most suitable here, meaning the cinema provides or presents a welcome escape.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: I shall take revenge ...... you.
explanation: The correct idiomatic expression is 'to take revenge on someone,' indicating the target of the revenge.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: This book is a useful ...... to our library.
explanation: A book that joins a collection like a library is referred to as an 'addition,' meaning something added to an existing group.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The boy was cured ...... typhoid.
explanation: The correct preposition to use with 'cured' when referring to a disease or illness is 'of.'

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The communalist represents the ...... of everything noble that we have inherited from our culture and history.
explanation: Antithesis means the direct opposite or a stark contrast to something, which fits the context of representing the opposite of noble values.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: He is so ...... to light that he never leaves the house without sunglasses.
explanation: Being 'sensitive to light' means experiencing discomfort or a strong reaction to it, hence the need for sunglasses.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The ...... of the Minister's statement cannot be verified by people who have no access to official records.
explanation: Veracity refers to the truthfulness, accuracy, or factual correctness of a statement, which is what would need to be verified against official records.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: But the introductory fee does not stop after the initial handshake, brokers have to ...... it up after each transaction.
explanation: Cough it up is an idiomatic expression meaning to pay money, often reluctantly or when it's due, which fits the context of brokers paying fees.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Colgate has also set an ambitious aim of ......on 8% value shared of tooth paste market by then end of first years.
explanation: Cornering the market is an idiom that means to gain sufficient control over a particular market to manipulate prices or dominate sales, aligning with aiming for a significant market share.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: ...... a failure of some traffic lights, traffic is moving very slowly.
explanation: Due to is a prepositional phrase used to introduce the reason or cause for something, making it suitable to explain why traffic is moving slowly.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The battalion operating from the mountain was able to ...... three enemy divisons.
explanation: In a military context, 'tie down' means to engage enemy forces, preventing them from being deployed elsewhere or from acting freely.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: I purposely ...... meet you during my last visit to Kashmir.
explanation: The phrase 'last visit to Kashmir' indicates a past event, so the simple past tense 'didn't' (did not) is the correct choice to describe a past, intentional action.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: I have read one novel by Premchand. I want to read ...... novel by him.
explanation: Another means 'one more' or 'an additional one,' which is appropriate when expressing a desire to read a second novel by the same author.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: A man remains narrow minded, self compliance and ignorant unless he visits other people and ...... from them.
explanation: Visiting other people allows one to gain new knowledge, perspectives, and understanding, thus 'learning' from them and combating ignorance.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: My father ...... down for a nap.
explanation: The verb 'to lie' (recline) is intransitive. While 'lies' or 'lay' would be correct for simple tenses, 'lie' is the base form of this intransitive verb, unlike 'lays' which is the transitive verb 'to lay' (to place).

subject: Verbal Ability
text: I think they allow their children too much ......
explanation: Latitude refers to the freedom to choose or act, or the scope for independent action, which fits the context of allowing children too much freedom.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Once you suspect a person of double dealing, you ought to keep him at arm's ......
explanation: To keep someone at arm's length is an idiom meaning to avoid becoming too friendly or involved with someone, often due to distrust.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Affix a revenue stamp and put your signature ...... it.
explanation: The correct preposition to use when signing a document or stamp is 'on,' indicating direct placement of the signature.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: During his long discourse, he did not touch that point.
explanation: The phrasal verb 'to touch on' (or 'touch upon') means to mention or deal with a subject briefly, which is the appropriate meaning in this context.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: He found a wooden broken chair in the room.
explanation: In English, adjectives describing condition (like 'broken') typically precede adjectives describing material (like 'wooden') when modifying a noun.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: He could not look anything in the dark room.
explanation: See refers to the ability to perceive with one's eyes, which is the appropriate verb when describing the inability to perceive objects in darkness. Look implies directing one's gaze.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The greatest thing in style is to have a use of metaphor.
explanation: Command of metaphor means having a masterful skill and control in using metaphors effectively, which is a greater stylistic achievement than merely having 'knowledge' of them.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: While crossing the highway a five year old child was knocked out by a passing car.
explanation: Knocked down is the correct phrasal verb to describe being hit by a vehicle and falling to the ground. Knocked out means rendered unconscious.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: hoping not to be disturbed, I sat down in my easy chair to read the book. I won as a prize .
options: I had won as a prize
I had to win as a prize
explanation: The past perfect tense 'had won' is necessary here to indicate that the action of winning the prize occurred before the past action of sitting down to read the book.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: More than one person was killed in accident.
explanation: The expression more than one + noun, though plural in sense, is followed by a singular verb. Example: More than one person has died in the accident. Explanation provided by: Samy Kumar . Updated on 4th Aug 2018.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: No one could explain how a calm and balanced person like him could penetrate such a mindless act on his friends.
explanation: To "perpetrate an act" means to carry out or commit it. The original word "penetrate" is incorrect in this context, as it means to go into or through something.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Five years ago today, I am sitting in a small Japanese car, driving across Poland towards Berlin.
explanation: The phrase "Five years ago today" clearly indicates a past event. Therefore, the present continuous "am sitting" should be replaced with a past tense, such as the past continuous "was sitting," to describe an action ongoing at that specific past moment.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: I took the cycle which he bought yesterday .
options: that which he had bought yesterday
explanation: Since the action of buying the cycle ("had bought") occurred before the action of taking it ("took"), the past perfect tense is required for the earlier event to correctly establish the sequence of past actions.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Please make it a point to send you letter at my address .
explanation: When sending something to a destination, the correct preposition to use with "address" is "to," indicating the direction or recipient. "At my address" refers to being present at a location.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: If you are living near a market place you should be ready to bear the disturbances caused by traffic.
explanation: The phrasal verb "to bear with" means to tolerate or endure something patiently. In this context, it correctly conveys the idea of being ready to accept and live with the disturbances caused by traffic.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: I hope you won't object to me watching while you work.
explanation: The verb "object to" is followed by a gerund. When the gerund has a subject (in this case, "I" or "me"), the possessive form of the pronoun ("my") should be used before the gerund.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: You cannot forbid him leaving .
explanation: The verb "forbid" is typically followed by an object pronoun and an infinitive verb (e.g., "forbid someone to do something"). Therefore, "forbid him to leave" is the correct grammatical construction.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: 20 kms are not a great distance in these days of fast moving vehicles.
explanation: When a plural noun refers to a single unit of measurement, time, or money, it takes a singular verb. Here, "20 kms" is considered a single distance, thus requiring the singular verb "is."

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The more they earn, more they spend .
options: More they earn, more they spend
More they earn, the more they spend
The more they earn, the more they spend
explanation: This sentence uses the correlative construction "The more... the more..." to express a proportional relationship between two clauses. Both parts of the comparison require the definite article "the."

subject: Verbal Ability
text: It became clear that the strangers were heading into a serious disaster.
explanation: The phrase "heading towards" is commonly used to indicate movement in the direction of a particular outcome or destination, especially when referring to an impending event like a disaster. "Heading into" can imply entering a physical space or state, but "towards" better conveys the sense of approaching an undesirable situation.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The dissidents hold a great problem in every political party.
explanation: The phrase "hold a problem" is not idiomatic. "Cause a problem" means to be the reason for a difficulty or issue, which fits the context of dissidents creating difficulties for a political party.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: I would have waited for you at the station if I knew that you would come.
explanation: This sentence expresses an unreal condition in the past (third conditional). The correct structure for the "if" clause is the past perfect tense ("if I had known") to match the "would have + past participle" in the main clause.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: They are social insects, living in communities , regulated by definite laws, each member of society bearing well-defined and separate part in the work of a colony.
explanation: The original sentence is grammatically correct and well-structured, using participial phrases ("living in communities," "regulated by definite laws") and an absolute phrase ("each member... bearing...") to provide descriptive information about the social insects. The provided options do not offer a clearer or more correct alternative.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Practically every part of the banana tree is used by man.
explanation: The phrase "Practically every part" is grammatically correct and effectively conveys that almost all components of the banana tree are utilized. The sentence requires no improvement.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: My opinion for the film is that it will bag the national ward.
explanation: The correct preposition to use with "opinion" when referring to the subject of that opinion is "about" or "on." "Opinion for" is grammatically incorrect in this context.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The end of the examinations is (an) oppurtunity for celebrating.
explanation: While "opportunity" (correctly spelled) is suitable, "moment" is also a fitting word to describe a specific point in time that is suitable for celebration. The options offer synonyms, and "moment" is a valid choice.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: We were not the wiser for all this effort to explain the case to us.
explanation: The idiomatic expression is "none the wiser," which means to know no more than before, despite having received an explanation or information. Replacing "not" with "none" makes the phrase grammatically correct and idiomatic.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Whenever my students come across new words, I ask them to look for them in the dictionary.
explanation: The phrasal verb "to look up" is used when referring to finding information, such as the meaning of a word, in a reference source like a dictionary. Since "words" is plural, "them" is the correct pronoun.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: We look forward to hear from you.
explanation: We look forward to hearing from you.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: It was indeed a shock for her, but she has later recovered from it.
explanation: The adverb "since" is correctly used with the present perfect tense ("has recovered") to indicate that an action or state began at a particular time in the past and has continued or been completed up to the present. It implies "from that time onward."

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Realising is the significance of technical education for developing country, the government laid aside a large sum on it during the last plan-period.
explanation: The phrasal verb "to set aside" means to reserve or save something, typically money or time, for a particular purpose. This is the most appropriate idiom for the government allocating funds for technical education.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: If you are not clear about the meaning of a word, it is wise to look to a dictionary.
explanation: The phrasal verb "to look up" is specifically used when referring to finding information, such as the meaning of a word, in a dictionary or other reference material.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: You are warned against committing the same mistake again.
explanation: The construction "to be warned against (doing something)" is grammatically correct and idiomatic, meaning to be cautioned not to perform a particular action. The sentence requires no improvement.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: No sooner he had returned home then his mother felt happy.
explanation: The correct correlative conjunction for "No sooner" is "than." Additionally, when "No sooner" begins a sentence, it requires inversion, meaning the auxiliary verb ("did" for simple past) comes before the subject.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: He should move on to the next point, and not harp one sting only .
explanation: The idiom is "to harp on one string," meaning to dwell tediously on a single subject. "Harp upon one string only" correctly uses the idiom with "upon" as an alternative to "on," and corrects the typo "sting" to "string."

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Either he or I am going .
options: he is going or I am
explanation: In an "either...or" construction, the verb agrees with the subject closest to it. Since "I" is closer to the verb, "am" is the correct form, making the original sentence grammatically sound.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: I hope you vividly remember the premier of the film when I, my wife and you were present in the hall.
explanation: The polite order for referring to multiple people, including oneself, is typically second person, then third person, then first person (you, he/she/they, I). Among the given options, "my wife, you and I" follows a polite order (third, second, first person).

subject: Verbal Ability
text: To get one's name in the Rowland Ward's book of hunting records was the hot ambition of every serious hunter.
explanation: The phrase "hot ambition" is not idiomatic. "Burning ambition" is a common and strong idiom that perfectly conveys an intense and passionate desire, making it the most appropriate improvement.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Taxpayers are to be conscious of their privileges.
explanation: The phrase "are to be" implies an expectation or duty. "Ought to be" (option 2) is the most appropriate improvement as it conveys a sense of moral obligation or advisability, suggesting that taxpayers should properly be aware of their privileges.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: As she was suffering from high fever, she could not face the examination.
explanation: The correct idiomatic expression for participating in an examination is "to take the examination." "Face the examination" is less common and "take" is the standard verb.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: The demonstration passed off peacefully.
explanation: The phrasal verb "passed off" correctly means that the demonstration happened or took place, and the adverb "peacefully" indicates it occurred without incident. Therefore, no improvement is needed.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Every time I go in a lift to my sixth floor apartment, I remember the calm and serenity of my ancestral home in the village.
explanation: The idiomatic expression for using an elevator or lift is "to take a lift" (or "take an elevator"). "Go in a lift" is less natural in standard English.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: In fact, if it hadn't been for his invaluable advice on so many occasions I wouldn't have achieved anything in life.
explanation: The phrase "invaluable advice" is grammatically correct and idiomatic. "Invaluable" means extremely useful or precious, and "advice" is an uncountable noun, so no improvement is needed.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Mr. Smith arrived at India in June last year.
explanation: When referring to arrival in a large geographical area like a country or city, the correct preposition is "in" (e.g., "arrived in India"). "Arrived at" is used for specific, smaller locations.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: But in all these cases conversion from scale have well-formulated .
explanation: The subject "conversion" is singular, requiring a singular verb. The original sentence uses "have," which is plural. Option 0, "can be well-formulated," correctly uses a singular modal verb phrase and completes the passive voice construction.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: With a thundering roar the huge rocket soared up from the launching pad.
explanation: The phrasal verb "took off" is the most appropriate and common expression to describe the initial launch or departure of a rocket from its launching pad.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: There is dearth of woman doctor in our state. We shall have to recruit some from the other states.
explanation: When "woman" or "man" acts as an adjective modifying a profession, both parts of the compound noun are pluralized. Therefore, "women doctors" is the correct plural form.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: If you cross the line you will be disqualified.
explanation: The phrase "cross the line" is a correct and common idiom meaning to exceed an acceptable limit or boundary. The sentence is grammatically sound and requires no improvement.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: Why the dinosaurs died out is not known .
explanation: The clause "Why the dinosaurs died out" functions as a noun clause, serving as the subject of the sentence. The construction "Why [subject] [verb] is not known" is grammatically correct and requires no improvement.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: His father won't be able to leave for Varnasi until they have arrived .
explanation: In clauses of time introduced by conjunctions like "until," the simple present tense is used to refer to future events, even when the main clause is in the future. Therefore, "until they arrive" is the correct and most natural phrasing.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: I will not go to school, if it shall rain tomorrow .
explanation: In a first conditional sentence, the if-clause (condition) uses the simple present tense to refer to a future possibility, while the main clause uses the simple future. Thus, "if it rains tomorrow" is the correct form.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: If I stood alone in defence of truth, and the whole world is banded against me and against truth. I would fight them all.
explanation: This is a second conditional sentence describing a hypothetical situation. The "if" clause requires a past tense verb ("stood"), and for consistency and hypothetical meaning, the second part of the condition should also be in the past subjunctive, "were banded."

subject: Verbal Ability
text: He has not and can never be in the good books of his employer because he lacks honesty.
options: has not and can never been
has not been and can never be
explanation: When two auxiliary verbs share a main verb, the main verb must be in a form that is grammatically correct for both. "Has not" requires "been" (present perfect), while "can never" requires the base form "be" (modal verb). Thus, "has not been and can never be" is the correct construction.

subject: Verbal Ability
text: When the examinations were over Anil and me went to our native town.
explanation: When "I" is part of a compound subject, it must be in the subjective case ("I"), not the objective case ("me"). Additionally, it is considered polite to refer to others before oneself, making "Anil and I" the correct and most appropriate choice.

subject: Verbal Ability
